questions.stem: Evolution is defined as a change in the:

options.text: Behaviour of a single organism in its lifetime
Size of an organism due to diet

questions.explanation: Evolution is change in the heritable characteristics of a population over generations.

questions.scenario: A weightlifter develops large muscles during their life.
questions.stem: Why is this NOT considered evolution?

options.text: It is an acquired change, not a heritable one

questions.explanation: Acquired changes that are not genetic are not passed on, so they are not evolution; this distinguishes Darwinism from Lamarckism.

questions.scenario: Two species have very similar DNA base sequences for a shared gene.
questions.stem: This is evidence that they:
questions.explanation: Similar DNA, RNA or protein sequences give powerful evidence of common ancestry.

questions.scenario: Many breeds of dog have been produced from wolves in a few thousand years.
questions.stem: This selective breeding provides evidence that:

options.text: All dogs are a different species from wolves

questions.explanation: The variety produced by selective breeding shows how rapidly evolutionary change can occur.

questions.scenario: The forelimbs of humans, whales and bats share the same basic bone arrangement.
questions.stem: These are examples of:
questions.explanation: The pentadactyl limb is a homologous structure, indicating descent from a common ancestor.

questions.stem: The classic example of a homologous structure used in the syllabus is the:
questions.explanation: The pentadactyl (five-digit) limb is the syllabus example of homologous structures.

questions.scenario: The wings of birds and the wings of insects both allow flight but evolved separately.
questions.stem: These are examples of:
questions.explanation: Analogous structures share a function but have different evolutionary origins, arising through convergent evolution.

questions.stem: Analogous structures have:

options.text: The same function but different evolutionary origins
The same origin but different functions

questions.explanation: Analogous structures perform the same function but arose from different evolutionary origins.

questions.stem: How do new species arise?

options.text: By the splitting of pre-existing species
By the merging of two species
By an individual acquiring new traits

questions.explanation: Speciation, the splitting of pre-existing species, is the only way new species have appeared.

questions.stem: Speciation increases the number of species on Earth, while the opposite effect is caused by:
questions.explanation: Speciation increases the total number of species and extinction decreases it.

questions.scenario: A single species slowly changes over time but does not split.
questions.stem: Is this speciation?

options.text: No, because no new species is formed by splitting

questions.explanation: Gradual change within one lineage is not speciation; speciation requires splitting into two or more species.

questions.stem: Two things are needed for speciation to occur:
questions.explanation: Speciation requires reproductive isolation together with differential selection acting on the separated populations.

questions.scenario: A river separates a population into two groups that can no longer meet.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: A physical barrier such as a river causes geographical isolation, a means of achieving reproductive isolation.

questions.scenario: Bonobos and common chimpanzees are separated by the Congo River.
questions.stem: This example illustrates divergence caused by:
questions.explanation: The Congo River geographically separates bonobos and chimpanzees, leading to divergence through differential selection.

questions.stem: Which of these would count as evolution?

options.text: A change in allele frequencies in a population over generations
A plant growing taller with more sunlight
An animal learning a new behaviour
A person tanning in the sun

questions.explanation: Evolution is a heritable change across generations, unlike acquired changes within one lifetime.

questions.stem: Amino acid sequences of a protein shared by many species can be used to:
questions.explanation: Amino acid and base sequences give powerful evidence of common ancestry and evolutionary relationships.

questions.scenario: Wild mustard has been bred into cabbage, broccoli and kale.
questions.stem: This is evidence for evolution from:
questions.explanation: Producing diverse crops from one wild species by selective breeding is evidence of how fast evolutionary change can occur.

questions.stem: Homologous structures provide evidence for evolution because they suggest:
questions.explanation: Homologous structures share a common underlying plan, indicating descent from a common ancestor.

questions.scenario: The streamlined body shapes of sharks (fish) and dolphins (mammals) are similar.
questions.stem: This similarity is an example of:
questions.explanation: Similar body shapes in distantly related animals adapted to the same environment are analogous, from convergent evolution.

questions.stem: Which statement about speciation is correct?

options.text: It is the only way new species have appeared
It decreases the number of species
It is the same as extinction

questions.explanation: Speciation by splitting is the only way new species have appeared, increasing the number of species.

questions.stem: Reproductive isolation is important for speciation because it:

options.text: Prevents gene flow between the diverging populations

questions.explanation: Reproductive isolation stops gene flow, allowing populations to diverge into separate species.

questions.scenario: Lamarck suggested giraffes got long necks by stretching during their lives.
questions.stem: Why does modern evolutionary theory reject this?

options.text: Giraffes do not have long necks

questions.explanation: Darwinian evolution acts on heritable variation; acquired changes like stretching are not passed to offspring.

questions.stem: Why is molecular sequence data considered strong evidence for evolution?

options.text: The degree of similarity reflects how closely species are related
Sequences are the same in all species
Sequences change randomly with no pattern

questions.explanation: More closely related species have more similar sequences, giving quantifiable evidence of common ancestry.

questions.stem: Convergent evolution occurs when organisms:

options.text: Independently evolve similar features under similar pressures
Inherit features from a shared ancestor

questions.explanation: Convergent evolution produces similar (analogous) features in unrelated organisms facing similar selection pressures.

questions.stem: Homologous structures may have different functions but share:

options.text: A common underlying structure and origin

questions.explanation: Homologous structures share a common origin and basic structure even when their functions differ.

questions.stem: Which process directly increases the total number of species on Earth?
questions.explanation: Speciation increases species number; extinction decreases it.

questions.scenario: Two isolated populations experience different environmental pressures.
questions.stem: The different pressures lead to:
questions.explanation: Different selection pressures on isolated populations cause differential selection, promoting divergence.

questions.stem: Selective breeding differs from natural selection because the selecting agent is:
questions.explanation: In selective breeding humans choose which organisms breed, showing how quickly heritable change can be produced.

questions.stem: Evolution acts on which level?

options.text: A single individual in one day

questions.explanation: Evolution is a change in the heritable characteristics of a population across generations.

questions.scenario: The theory of evolution by natural selection explains a huge range of observations.
questions.stem: Why is it still called a 'theory'?

options.text: It cannot be formally proven true, though it is strongly supported

questions.explanation: It is a well-supported, pragmatic truth called a theory because science cannot formally prove it true by correspondence.

questions.stem: Which pair are analogous rather than homologous structures?

options.text: A butterfly wing and a bird wing
A human arm and a whale flipper
A bat wing and a cat leg
A human hand and a mole forelimb

questions.explanation: Butterfly and bird wings share function but not evolutionary origin, so they are analogous; the others are homologous pentadactyl limbs.

questions.stem: For speciation to be complete, the two new populations must be:
questions.explanation: Complete speciation requires reproductive isolation so the populations no longer interbreed.

questions.stem: The pentadactyl limb is found in amphibians, reptiles, birds and mammals. This suggests they:

options.text: Descended from a common ancestor with this limb
Evolved the limb separately by chance

questions.explanation: A shared homologous limb structure across these groups indicates descent from a common ancestor.

questions.stem: Geographical isolation contributes to speciation by:

options.text: Physically separating populations so they cannot interbreed

questions.explanation: Geographical isolation is a way of achieving the reproductive isolation needed for speciation.

questions.stem: The differences between a domestic breed and its wild ancestor demonstrate that:

options.text: Heritable change can accumulate quickly under selection
Wild and domestic forms are identical

questions.explanation: The rapid divergence of breeds from wild ancestors shows how quickly heritable change can occur under selection.

questions.stem: Which best explains why analogous structures do NOT indicate close relationship?

options.text: They arose independently, not from a shared ancestor
They come from the same ancestor

questions.explanation: Analogous structures evolved independently, so shared function does not indicate close common ancestry.

questions.stem: Which of the following changes could be inherited and so contribute to evolution?

options.text: A mutation in a reproductive cell

questions.explanation: Only heritable (genetic) changes, such as mutations in gametes, can contribute to evolution.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly links speciation and biodiversity?

options.text: Speciation increases biodiversity by adding species
Speciation has no effect on species number

questions.explanation: By adding new species, speciation increases the total number of species and hence biodiversity.

questions.stem: Which combination is required for two isolated populations to become separate species?

options.text: Free gene flow plus identical environments

questions.explanation: Both reproductive isolation and differential selection are needed for populations to diverge into new species.

questions.stem: Base sequences, amino acid sequences and homologous structures all provide:

options.text: Independent lines of evidence for evolution
Proof that species do not change

questions.explanation: Multiple independent lines of evidence, molecular and structural, all support evolution and common ancestry.

questions.scenario: Speciation occurs while populations still share the same geographic area.
questions.stem: This is called:
questions.explanation: Sympatric speciation happens without geographic separation, while allopatric speciation involves geographic isolation.

questions.scenario: A population is split by a mountain range and diverges into two species.
questions.stem: This is an example of:
questions.explanation: Allopatric speciation occurs when populations are geographically separated, as by a mountain range.

questions.scenario: Two populations breed at different times of year and so never mate.
questions.stem: This is which type of reproductive isolation?
questions.explanation: Reproductive isolation can be geographic, behavioural or temporal; breeding at different times is temporal isolation.

questions.scenario: One ancestral species rapidly gives rise to many species that fill different niches.
questions.stem: This process is called:
questions.explanation: Adaptive radiation is the rapid diversification of one lineage into many species filling different niches, increasing biodiversity.

questions.stem: Adaptive radiation increases biodiversity by allowing related species to:

options.text: Coexist without competing by using vacant niches
Compete for exactly the same niche

questions.explanation: Adaptive radiation lets closely related species occupy vacant niches and coexist, boosting biodiversity.

questions.scenario: In many animal species, elaborate courtship behaviour must occur before mating.
questions.stem: How does courtship behaviour prevent hybridization?

options.text: Members of different species do not recognise each other's courtship signals
It forces different species to mate
It has no effect on mating

questions.explanation: Species-specific courtship behaviour acts as a barrier to hybridization, since other species do not respond to it.

questions.scenario: A mule is produced by crossing a horse and a donkey.
questions.stem: The mule is an example of:
questions.explanation: The mule is a sterile interspecific hybrid, illustrating a barrier that prevents allele mixing between species.

questions.scenario: In some plants, a new species forms in a single generation through hybridization and doubling of chromosomes.
questions.stem: This process is called:
questions.explanation: Hybridization combined with polyploidy can produce a new plant species abruptly, in as little as one generation.

questions.stem: Which genus is used as an example of abrupt speciation by hybridization and polyploidy?
questions.explanation: Persicaria (knotweed or smartweed) is used as an example because it contains many species formed by these processes.

questions.stem: Which is a similarity between sympatric and allopatric speciation?

options.text: Both require reproductive isolation to occur
Both always require a geographic barrier

questions.explanation: Both types of speciation require reproductive isolation; they differ in whether a geographic barrier is involved.
